speaker: interviewer
text: Today is December 11, 1996. I'm interviewing Eva Lax Slomovits, and this is tape number eight of the interview. Can you tell me a little bit more about the life in the DP camp during those three years?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the DP camp, actually, most of the people didn't work. And being it was the English, a lot of people tried to go to Israel. So they would go-- not legally-- go to Italy, the young boys and girls. They were going to It-- there were people that came to the DP camp from Israel, from the Haganah. And they were trying to organize girls and boys to go to Eng-- Italy. And from Italy, they said they would take a boat and go to Israel. And a lot of people did go and-- from our DP camp. There was the-- the boat, the Exodus. It was from our DP camp, the girls and boys that went on this journey.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, what we-- we decided, once we found out that my sister is-- you know, with my sister, we want to come back. We want to be united with my sister. Because that was our dream, always, to come to America. So in-- in the DP camp, most people didn't work. I worked because I chose to work because of the kosher kitchen. But most of the people didn't work. So they didn't do-- some-- some boys did some black market. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The girls-- and then they start dating, and getting married. And my two sisters got married in-- in DP camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then one of my sisters had a daughter, her daughter, there in DP camp, which it was-- she was, like, the second baby born after the war. A lot of people didn't think that we will be able to ever to have children anymore, because they said that we got some medication in Auschwitz that we wouldn't be able to have children.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when these children were born, we were very excited that there are-- a new gen-- that we will be able to have children, we will be-- continue to have Jewish children. And this rabbi organized a [NON-ENGLISH]. I belonged to the [NON-ENGLISH]. And there was also a boys, a [INAUDIBLE], and he was teaching how to pray and how to-- we used to go Shabbas, every Saturday, to shul, and observing.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the weddings were very according to orthodox. You know, most of the girls that got married there, they covered their hair and they-- and we start going back to-- to the life that we-- we knew from before the war. And already, in the DP camp, we started the Jewish-- continued the Jewish education and life in-- in the DP camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your sisters' weddings?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes. Very well. I remember. I did all the cooking, and I was so tired. [LAUGHS] And I remember it very well. I-- I did all the weddings, not only my sisters', the cooking.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were the caterer?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was the caterer. That's right. I was cooking for-- if I was there cooking, then the rabbi ate. If I wasn't there, if somebody else was-- cooked, the rabbi wouldn't eat. He trusted me completely, that I do the right thing, kosher. [INAUDIBLE]. He knew, because I told him, the family that I was raised. So-- and he used to come in the kitchen. He used to see that we only had flesh meat for lunch, we served. And so I chose to-- to-- to work, because I felt it was a very important thing to have a kosher kitchen. I believed very strongly in that-- to have a kosher kitchen.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned the schools. Were they able to build the synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. The rabbi went to England, and he brought a Torah. And now that we had a Torah, we had a place where to put the Torah. So we took a kitchen, and it was near-- in a-- in a building. There was a-- like, a Jewish-- a club. They made it-- we used to get together there, the boys, the girls, the [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was a kitchen. Nobody used that kitchen, because nobody lived in that building except the club was there. So that kitchen, the boys made a-- for the Torah, with, like, a closet to put in. And we, the girls, would, by hand, we made curtains with the Jewish stars, the white--

speaker: interviewee
text: We took a sheet, a white sheet. We found some blue-- rib-- not rib-- like ribbon, and we sewed it on, a star of David. And I'm going to show you. I have a picture of the shul that we made. And I remember the first-- very first time where we came to shul, and they took out the Sefer Torah. And how we cried, and how we-- to live to see, again, a shul. And to be near a Sefer Torah, and to be able to-- to pray of our choice. We didn't have to hide anymore. But it wasn't easy. It was still very difficult for us there, in-- in the DP camp. Because, like I said, we were afraid to walk in the streets between the Germans. And we were still free and not free. Still not free.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you come to America?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came in March '28 at-- 19-- 1948. March the 3rd, 1948, I came to the shores by boat. I came-- took two weeks on a-- on a small boat. We were so sick, I thought we're going to die. I came with my sister and her husband and the baby that was born in the DP camp. And my sis-- my other sister, Ilona, she was already here. She came before, because she came on the Polish quota. We came on the Czech quota, so it took longer for us to get-- she got married, took a Polish boy-- Jewish boy. So he-- she came here before. And we still had to wait for the quota. So we were from Czechoslovakia. The Czech quota was slower. So we came later, a year later. So I-- I remember, when we came, they waited for us. So my sister, Lilly, went to live with my sister, Ilona, because she had a two-bedroom apartment. She had a baby. And I went to live with my sister that came-- that was here a long time. So I was only about eight years old when my sister, Cilia, left to America. I was a little girl. And she cried. She said, my god, I expected a little girl coming. And here I was 18 years old, 19 years already, when I came to America.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of life did you start to lead at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, at the beginning, I couldn't go to work. I was sick. I had TB and I was sick. But then when I got better, I-- I went to-- because I came through the United Jewish Appeal. They paid for my ticket, and-- and they brought me out.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they had where they advertised-- a company that advertised for UJA, they took in some people to work for them. Whatever work I could do. You know, I couldn't speak the language. I couldn't-- so but I-- they explained me what to do, and I was able to do, in an advertising firm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I registered to go to school. I want to go to school very badly. I always liked school, to me. And I registered at night school. I worked in the daytime and went at night to school. And I learn-- to learn the language and the custom of the country. And I want to be Americanized. I want to be like all the American girls. I-- I want to adjust to the country. And I loved everything about America. It was just very hard for me to come here. But once I was here, I-- I loved everything. There was-- nothing was too-- too-- even we didn't have it easy. We had it very tough, but we were free. But even for-- to freedom, you have to get used to freedom. I used to go into the bank and I used to see a guard there. And I used to jump. I says, oh my God. Then I realize that I'm in America. Anybody in a uniform would scare me. I would get scared. A conductor. I never want to go on the first train, because the conductor was there. Even I knew, and I still didn't want to expose myself to somebody with a uniform. Policemen in the street, I would get scared. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You have to get used to freedom. But I-- I loved it. I got used to it very fast. I loved everything about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that your two sisters got married in the DP camps.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you marry?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I got married here. I came here, and I met my husband here.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you meet him?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to see somebody that I knew from Germany. He came, and I went with my brother-in-law, and he was his friend. So he introduced me to him, and we started going out. He needed a home, and I needed a home. I really-- he-- we really fell in love. It wasn't just that we needed a home. But we were looking to settle down. And he was working as a butcher, and I was working in a-- already, at that time, in a factory, making jackets-- boys jackets. And I made, already, a good salary. I made already $50. That was my top salary. And he made and we felt we-- we could manage on that. We took a furnished room and we got married. We got married in 1952.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your husband's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Louis Slomovits. His Jewish name is Lazar.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you start a family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we start a family right away. We-- we had a-- our-- our daughter was born in 1953, on May 16, 1953.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is Miriam. And then we want to have more children, so three years later. It was very difficult, but we always helped, the sisters, one with the other, you know, to babysit. And we didn't go places. We just stayed home. And I used to help my husband, though. I used to take her down in the carriage I lived not far from. My husband was a butcher. He had a butcher store. He bought a butcher store by then. And I used to take her down to the store and help my husband. And then we had, in 1956, March 28, we-- a boy was born to us. And this boy was born to the family, the Slomovits family, like a messiah would come. Because he was the first boy born in the family, the-- a male, named after my husband's father, which he died in Europe in 1936.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the whole family was very excited about my-- my son. His name was Boris Slomovits, like their father. So he was a little spoiled brat, too. Have you had any other children?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Just these two children.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do your children have children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My daughter got married and she has-- 15 years ago. She has two children. Her oldest boy is named after my two brothers, Eliot Mikhail. And my little-- the girl is 12 years. She's-- her name is Jessica. She's named after her husband's family. And my son got married, and he was married one year, and he got divorced.

speaker: interviewer
text: What work have you done since you've come to-- after the coat factory? What other work have you done since then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I always helped my husband. He was-- when he was in the butcher store, I helped my husband in the butcher store. And then when he-- after that, we moved to California. So in California, I didn't work. Because he-- his work, his place was 50 miles from Los Angeles. And I didn't want to go to-- move to Los Angeles at all, but he insisted we should go there, that it's waiting for him. They are a big business. So he wanted to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I made a pact with him. The only way I go, if I could live in Los Angeles, because in Los Angeles, I could send my kids to yeshiva. And where the-- his store was, there were only Mexican people living. And I didn't want to raise my children between Mexicans. I want to raise my children with-- they should go to yeshiva. So he promised he's going to do that for me. So we stayed in Los Angeles for three years, and-- three and a half years. The kids started yeshiva. They went to yeshiva there. But it was too hard for him to travel every day, so he wouldn't come home every night. He would come, like, every other night. He-- he was with partners.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I hated it. I didn't have my husband. I didn't have my sisters. I-- I want to come back to New York. And I told my husband. I said, the first chance you have, I-- please. I want you should take me back to New York. And something happened between the partnership, and they bought him out. And I says, now we're going back to New York. And we came back to New York, and I was the happiest to be back in New York with my family again, with my sisters. And the children, too. They grew up together. We always lived in the same neighborhood. And they grew up like sisters and brothers. And now, even, the children are very close, which we were very happy about-- my sister's children and my children. And they live, now-- they moved out to the five towns, but all the youngsters live around there, and they're very close. They're like sisters. And then when my husband came here, he went to-- into jewelry business. Completely different. So I worked with him in the jewelry business. And my son went to college two years, and he had no interest in taking up something in school. So he went into business with my husband, and he stayed with my business-- in the business with my husband. And my daughter went to school. She continued her education. She went to Brooklyn College, and after that she went to Hofstra for her PhD she got in psychology, and now she has her own practice. She married somebody that is a dentist, and he has his own practice on Wall Street. And thank God. I-- this-- this is-- this is the beauty of my-- of my life, that I survived. And if it was ever answered, why did I survive, I survived to raise a family, have a family, and continue. What my father taught me, I taught my-- my children. How to be Jewish, how to love other people, not to hate. To live on love, and not on hate, because hate doesn't bring nice things, and love is always nice. And no matter what kind of people they are, you have to love them and respect them for their belief. And you have to believe. You have to know what-- what you-- where you belong in the Jewish community. They're very active in the Jewish community, with the second-generation survivors, my daughter. And I'm very proud of my children, the way-- the way they grew up, and of my nieces and nephews.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're a very close family, and they all have high education, and-- not only in-- in the Jewish world, but in-- also all of them have master's, doctorates. And the whole family is, thank God, very-- married and families. And we're very proud of them. So if ever, my-- if I ask myself a question, why did I live through? This is my answer, to raise a family. Before we end, do you have any final thoughts about your experience, what you've gone through?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, what I want to say is [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what I want to bring out is hate. They-- they-- they hated the Jews, and that's why they brought this tragedy on the Jewish people. And I always feel, if we would have had an Israel, we wouldn't have a Holocaust. Because somebody would speak up for us. Or, after the war, if we would have had an Israel, we would have had a place to go. And I know that all the doors were closed for the survivors. There were-- nobody made an exception to take in the survivors. And even the United States, which I love them, I accomplished a lot, no other country would give me what the United States gave me. And I appreciate and I love the country, the freedom. But I can't forgive them that they didn't make an exception to take in some survivors, that we had to be three years in a DP camp between Germans that hated us. They looked just to kill us again. And that we have to be on guard and watch democracy, but also watch Israel. Because I feel that the Jewish people need Israel very badly. Because if we would have had an Israel, we wouldn't have a Holocaust, and we would have had a home to go to.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else? OK, well, I want-- I want to thank you for allowing us to interview you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I thank you very much for doing it, and I'm glad I did it. Because it wasn't easy, but I feel, like my children say, you owe it to us. And I don't want to disappoint my children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Eva, who's in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my father. And this picture was taken in 1938 for his passport to come to America.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is his name, again?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name is Yitchak Lax.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's in this picture, Eva?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is taken in 1938 from my family to send to my sister, Celia, to America. And standing from left to right, the first one is my youngest brother, Eliahou. The second one is Lilly. Then is my father, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and that standing is Michael, Mikhail. In the back am I, [INAUDIBLE]. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's me, with the braids. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and this is my sister, Ilona. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is my brother, Michael. The-- he's younger than I am, but he's older from the two brothers. The picture was taken at [? Irsava ?] in 1938 to send it here to America to my sister Celia. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is my youngest brother, Eliahou. And he-- this picture was also taken in [? Irsava ?] in 1938, and send it to my sister, Celia, to America. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This picture is taken in 1945 in Bergen-Belsen. The background is the barracks from Bergen-Belsen. And standing from left to right is me, [INAUDIBLE], and then a friend of ours, [? Adla, ?] from Romania, and my sister, Ilona. And that little girl didn't have a name, so we named her Sorah. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This picture was also taken in Bergen-Belsen. We went out all the-- from our DP camp to Bergen-Belsen in the summer time, like an outing. And it was very pleasant to be with all my friends, there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where are you seated here? Or are you sitting or standing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Over here, standing over here. Standing next to this very tall man. He-- that was my brother-in-law. He passed away. But I was standing in front of my brother-in-law. I am standing there. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is in Celle, the-- the [NON-ENGLISH] that I joined. There was a rabbi that made-- Rabbi [? Olefsky, ?] and he was instrumental in putting together [NON-ENGLISH]. And I am sitting the third on the left. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I'm sitting there. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is a-- a picture from the first shul after the war. Rabbi [? Olfesky ?] got a Sefer Torah. And you see on the very left corner, there is a kitchen cabinet yet, and we made-- the girls made, from a sheet, the cover for the-- for the Sefer Torahs, and the boys made the walking up to the Sefer Torah. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is a New Year's card, which says [NON-ENGLISH]. And I made it in Bergen-Belsen. I went to Bergen-Belsen, and I send it to my sister, here, to America. Which she was very happy to see this beautiful picture of me after the war. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] This picture was taken in 19-- 1990. I returned back home to look for something left. There was nothing left there except that big rock that I'm sitting in front of it, and got very emotional that there is nothing left but a rock from my youth.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's standing next to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- next to me is my sister Lilly. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is a book that was written by the name of David Adler for the Holocaust-- to-- to teach about the Holocaust children. And the name is And Eli is my brother, my youngest brother. And David Adler came to interview me about children that didn't survive, and he wrote this book. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] This is a picture that was taken at my sister Ilona's 36th wedding anniversary. And we're the four sisters. Every time there is an occasion, we would take, the four sisters, a picture together. My-- on my left am I, standing. And next to me is Ilona. And then it's my sister Celia that is not with us anymore. She passed away 10 years ago. And my sister Lilly.

speaker: interviewer
text: So every time there is some special function, we take pictures, the four sisters. We would take four sisters picture. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] This picture is my son, [? Baruch. ?] Bar-- we call him Barry. I call him [NON-ENGLISH]. And this was taken at my daughter's wedding, 15 years ago.
